VO_EN: The man across the table is laughing at something you said.
His hand is warm when he shakes yours.
He thinks you're a consultant from Munich.
He thinks you went to the same university.
He thinks you like the same wine.
But he believes all of it.
Every word he says to you goes into a file he'll never see.
You've never told anyone who you are.
Not the woman you date as cover.
Not even the man beside you on the plane back to Tel Aviv.
Your name changes with the passport.
Your accent changes with the city.
Your life changes with the briefing.
The only thing that stays is the watch on your left wrist.
Just time. That's all you're allowed to keep.
You've spent three years in an IDF reconnaissance unit.
You're good at things most people never learn.
One afternoon a phone rings in the barracks.
A voice you don't recognize asks if you're available for a meeting.
Just an address in Tel Aviv and a time.
You go. You don't know why yet.
The building has no sign on the door.
They put you in rooms with strangers and tell you to become someone else.
They give you a name, a backstory, a childhood you never had.
You walk into a cafe in Haifa and order coffee as a man named Daniel.
They test whether you flinch when someone calls a name that isn't yours.
They test whether you can lie to someone's face while they're being kind to you.
There's a woman in the third round who brings you coffee and asks about your mother.
You tell her a story about a woman who doesn't exist.
They teach you how to spot a tail on a crowded street.
How to recruit a source who doesn't want to be recruited.
How to build a cover identity that holds under pressure.
You practice dead drops in parking garages.
You memorize faces from photographs that are destroyed after you see them.
You forget which accent belongs to which name.
There's a moment during week fourteen where your instructor calls your real name across the hall.
This is the part they don't put in the movies.
It's designed to make lying feel like breathing.
Three weeks in Prague under a name you chose yourself.
You rent an apartment. You build a social circle.
You eat dinner with people who believe every word you say.
By the end of the third week you respond to the fake name faster than your real one.
Nothing proves you were ever here.
Your instructor shakes your hand and says one sentence.
You have business cards with a number that routes to a desk in Tel Aviv.
Your apartment is leased under a company that doesn't exist.
The bathroom smells like pine cleaner. You notice these things now.
You drink coffee at the same cafe every morning.
You tip well. You smile at the barista.
Your first target is a man named Friedrich.
He works at a research institute with access to things your country needs to know.
You meet him at a conference.
You think you understand why that makes it easier.
Over the next four months you become his closest friend.
His birthday, you bring a bottle of wine he mentioned once in passing.
You remembered because it was in the file.
Friedrich tells you about his daughter's illness.
He tells you about his failing marriage.
He tells you about a project at work he isn't supposed to discuss.
You listen. You nod. You say the right things.
You try to explain it to yourself later.
Later, in the safe house, you type every word into a report.
You mark it CLASSIFIED and send it to Tel Aviv.
You wash your hands and go to bed.
You don't know why you wash your hands.
Your wife's voice sounds different each time.
Abba, when are you coming home?
You've said soon for eleven months.
She tells you about a drawing she made at school.
She says it's because you're far away.
You hang up and sit in Martin Kessler's apartment.
The watch on your wrist ticks.
You're not sure whose time it's keeping.
A man you've never met leaves a car in a parking garage with the keys under the mat.
A dentist in the third district treats your tooth under a name that doesn't match your face.
A woman rents an apartment for you and never asks a single question.
They do it because they believe in something larger than you.
You use their loyalty like fuel.
You think about the man who left the car sometimes.
What he tells his wife when she asks where it went.
She's four. Same age as yours.
She asks if you want to see her room.
There are drawings on the wall. Stick figures. A family.
You stand there for three seconds too long.
The crayon box on her desk has teeth marks on one end.
Martin, come, the wine is open.
Your phone buzzes in your jacket pocket.
You excuse yourself to the bathroom.
You look at yourself in the mirror.
Your jaw is tight. You're not sure when that started.
You flush the toilet for the sound.
You walk back to the table.
You laugh at Friedrich's joke about his neighbor.
Nobody at the table notices anything.
You could stand up right now and say Friedrich, my name is not Martin.
I work for a foreign intelligence service.
Everything I told you is a lie.
Nobody's holding a gun to your head.
This is a dining room with drawings on the wall and a child's laughter in the next room.
Your hand moves toward the glass. For half a second your fingers don't grip. They tremble.
You choose to finish the wine.
You steer the conversation to the project.
He has no reason not to.
His daughter comes back from the hallway with a drawing she made for you.
Two people at a table. One is smiling.
You fold it and put it in your pocket.
You say goodnight at the door.
His daughter waves from the window.
You walk to your car and don't look back.
The drawing is still in your pocket.
The next morning your apartment is empty.
Two weeks later you read a short article in a German newspaper.
His daughter's drawings are still on the wall of an empty house.
You stop counting after the seventh.
The faces change but the pattern doesn't.
Somewhere around the fourth you stop washing your hands after.
You don't remember when that became normal.
The only thing that doesn't change is the watch on your wrist.
Between assignments you go home to Israel.
She runs to her mother when you walk through the door.
Your wife looks at you the way you look at a stranger on a train.
I don't know who you are anymore.
You want to say that makes two of us.
You stand in the kitchen for a while, looking at the tiles.
The grout is cracking in one corner. You notice that.
Not dramatically. You just stop going.
Your handler shakes your hand and says be well.
You almost ask him if any of it mattered.
You sit in a cafe in Tel Aviv.
A stranger behind you says the name Martin.
Then you realize he's talking to someone else.
Everything blurs for a second - the cafe, the street, the cup in your hand - then comes back.
You hold the coffee cup tighter.
You don't know whose hands these are.
The therapist asks about your work history.
She asks if anything traumatic happened.
Not because you don't want to.
You notice she holds her pen the same way Friedrich did.
Family photos. His daughter at a birthday party.
You don't like thinking about any of this.
You look at the exits out of habit.
You count the people near the door.
You notice the man two tables over checked his phone three times in two minutes.
But your hands are still scanning.
Your eyes are still reading the room.
You look down at your wrist.
Same one. No brand. No markings.
The only honest thing you ever carried.
You lived two lives for a country that will never say your name.
You built trust for a living.
And now you sit alone in a cafe with hands that can't stop reading the room.
You don't know which life was real.
If you ever wondered what it costs to make someone believe in you.
